Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered because I don't have internet at home, so I had to plan everything around when I could get to the library or a friend's place with wifi. I'd usually try to batch my studying - downloading materials when I had access, then working through them later. I spent about 18 hours a week studying, but a lot of that was listening to recorded lectures on my phone during my commute or while doing other things. Being an auditory learner, I found that podcasts and lecture recordings worked better for me than reading anyway.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on when I could actually get online access. Some weeks I'd have a burst of activity when I managed to get to the library, then nothing for days. Deadlines definitely pushed me to find internet access somehow, even if it meant staying late somewhere. I tried to keep some consistency with my offline studying, but the online part was always unpredictable based on my circumstances.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline approaching or knowing I needed to download new materials before losing access again. Sometimes my peers would message me that something important was posted, and I'd make the effort to log in. I also checked in when I knew discussion posts were due since those counted for participation. Honestly, without reliable internet, every login had to be purposeful - I couldn't just casually browse.
Q4: Not really a normal routine since my access was so inconsistent. What became routine was my workaround system - I'd mentally prepare a list of everything I needed to do online before I got access. When I finally logged in, I'd work through that list systematically. The routine was more about maximizing my limited online time than regular engagement with the platform itself.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly valuable because I could download them and watch offline, sometimes multiple times. I watched over 200 minutes of video content, and being an auditory learner, hearing explanations really helped concepts stick. The discussion forums were also useful because interacting with peers helped me understand different perspectives. I tried to be active there when I could, posting about 11 times and engaging with classmates.
Q6: I often delayed or skipped reading materials that were only available online without download options. Interactive quizzes that required constant internet connection were frustrating, so I'd put those off. Some supplementary resources just weren't practical for my situation, so I had to prioritize what I could actually access and use offline. It wasn't that I didn't want to use them - I just couldn't.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could either complete it during my limited online window or take it with me to study offline. Anything that directly connected to assignments or exams was priority. I also valued activities where I could hear explanations - lectures, audio content, discussions - because that's how I learn best. If something felt like busywork that wouldn't help my actual understanding, I'd skip it to focus on what mattered.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford given my access limitations. Required activities got priority because they affected my grade directly. But honestly, some optional stuff like extra discussion threads actually helped me learn more than some required readings. I just had to be strategic about what I could realistically engage with given my constraints.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them repeatedly on my phone. I borrowed textbooks from the library and took extensive handwritten notes. My friends and I formed a study group where we'd meet in person and discuss concepts - that peer interaction was huge for me. A lot of my 18 study hours happened completely outside the LMS.
Q10: When I was reviewing downloaded materials at home, listening to saved lectures during my commute, or meeting with my study group - none of that showed up anywhere. I'd spend hours working through problems with peers, explaining concepts to each other, and that collaborative learning was some of my most valuable study time. The LMS only saw me when I had internet, which was maybe a third of my actual engagement.
Q11: All the time - that was basically my standard approach. I'd access a video lecture once, download it, and then watch it three or four times offline over the following days. Same with any PDFs or materials I could save. One login might represent a week's worth of subsequent studying. My actual engagement with the content far exceeded what any single access timestamp would suggest.
Q12: There were definitely hard stretches, especially when I couldn't get online for days and felt like I was falling behind. Sometimes the sadness would creep in when I saw my attendance dropping and assignments piling up despite my efforts. The mindfulness exercises they suggested helped me stay grounded and not spiral into panic. My resilience kept me going, but there were moments when the barriers felt overwhelming.
Q13: The lack of internet was my biggest competing responsibility in a way - managing around it took so much mental energy. When I was tired or stressed, the extra effort to find internet access felt like too much. Sometimes I'd have the motivation to study but no way to submit work or participate online. The confusion came when course materials assumed constant connectivity that I just didn't have.
Q14: Sometimes I'd finally get online and just feel too mentally exhausted to really absorb anything. I'd go through the motions - clicking through pages, technically watching videos - but my mind wasn't fully there. After struggling to get access, sometimes I was too drained to actually engage meaningfully with the content. Those sessions probably looked like activity but weren't real learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, and my completion rate shows that. Part of it was access issues, but I'll be honest - sometimes I avoided things because I felt behind and overwhelmed. The sadness would make it hard to face work I knew I was struggling with. Even knowing something was important, the emotional weight of my situation sometimes made avoidance feel easier in the moment.
Q16: Deadlines were the main driver of when I'd make the extra effort to get online. I'd plan my library visits around due dates and try to submit things with whatever time I had. But sometimes deadlines passed while I was still trying to get access, which was incredibly frustrating. The deadline pressure helped me prioritize but also created stress when my circumstances didn't cooperate.
Q17: When I got feedback, it motivated me to engage more, especially positive feedback on my assignment work. My assignment scores were decent when I could actually submit things, which encouraged me. But feedback often came when I couldn't access it immediately, so there'd be a delay before I could respond or adjust. I wished I could have had more real-time interaction with instructors.
Q18: The course structure assumed a level of consistent online access that didn't match my reality. Weekly deadlines were tough when my access was unpredictable. I appreciated that some materials could be downloaded, but the structure didn't really accommodate students like me. The nine online courses I was taking all had similar assumptions, which compounded the challenge.
Q19: Downloadable content made engagement much easier - I could actually work with it. Discussion forums were good because I could compose responses offline and post when I got access. What made it harder was anything requiring real-time participation or constant connectivity. Timed assessments were particularly stressful because I couldn't always control when I'd have stable internet.
Q20: I think those students might be like me - spending time trying to access and download materials, navigating the platform, preparing for work they'll do offline. High activity could mean someone is struggling to find what they need or dealing with technical issues. It could also be someone who's engaging with content but facing barriers to actually completing and submitting assignments.
Q21: That definitely resonates with me, though I didn't perform as well as I hoped. I know students who barely logged in but studied extensively from downloaded materials or shared notes. The LMS activity is just one piece of the puzzle. Someone with good offline study habits and strong peer support might not need to be constantly logged in to learn effectively.
Q22: Course logs can show when someone accessed materials and basic participation patterns. But they completely miss offline studying, peer collaboration, and the quality of engagement during logged time. They can't capture someone listening to a downloaded lecture five times or spending hours discussing concepts with classmates. The logs show presence, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely trying to understand the material and connect with the learning community, regardless of what platform data shows. It's about intellectual curiosity, persistence through challenges, and finding ways to learn even when circumstances are difficult. For me, engagement meant listening to lectures repeatedly, discussing with peers, and keeping my motivation alive despite barriers. It's an internal commitment that doesn't always translate to clicks and timestamps.
Q24: The LMS would never show my resilience - how I kept going despite failing, despite the sadness, despite the access barriers. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with my study group or listening to lectures on the bus. It can't measure how the mindfulness intervention helped me manage my emotions and stay committed. My engagement was real and effortful, even if my final grade and login frequency don't reflect that.
